questions.question: Who is the author of 'Three Days to See'?
questions.explanation: Helen Keller, who was both deaf and blind, wrote this essay to share her perspective on the value of sight.

questions.question: What would Helen Keller do on her first day of sight?
questions.correct_answer: See the people who made her life worth living
questions.explanation: She wanted to look into the faces of her friends and her teacher, Mrs. Anne Sullivan Macy.

questions.question: What was the focus of the second day of her imagined sight?
questions.correct_answer: The history of the world and nature
questions.explanation: She planned to visit museums and theaters to see the pageant of human progress and the beauty of art.

questions.question: How would Helen spend her third and final day?
questions.correct_answer: Observing the workaday world in the city
questions.explanation: She wanted to see the hustle and bustle of New York City and the lives of ordinary people.

questions.question: What is the 'central lesson' Helen Keller wishes to teach?
questions.correct_answer: Use your senses as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind
questions.explanation: She urges people to appreciate their faculties and not take them for granted.

questions.question: What did Helen notice about her 'seeing' friends?
questions.correct_answer: They often saw very little despite having sight
questions.explanation: When she asked a friend what she observed in the woods, the friend replied, 'Nothing in particular.'

questions.question: Why did Helen want to visit the Metropolitan Museum of Art?
questions.correct_answer: To see the spirit of mankind shown through art
questions.explanation: She wanted to compress the history of human creativity into a single afternoon.

questions.question: What does Helen mean by the 'darkness' that would return?
questions.explanation: The three days were a temporary gift in her imagination before returning to her silent, dark world.

questions.question: Which sense did Helen say was the most delightful of all?
questions.explanation: Despite her other keen senses, she believed sight revealed the most beauty and soul.

questions.question: How does the essay change the reader's perspective on daily life?
questions.correct_answer: By encouraging gratitude for small details
questions.explanation: It reminds us that the things we find mundane are miracles to those who cannot experience them.

notes.title: Day 1: Personal Connections
notes.content: Focuses on the faces of loved ones, the home environment, and a walk in the woods.

notes.title: Day 2: Culture and History
notes.content: A journey through museums (Metropolitan) and the theater to experience human expression.

notes.title: Day 3: The Present World
notes.content: Exploring the city, slums, factories, and parks to see the reality of human existence.

notes.title: Theme of Gratitude
notes.content: The essay is a powerful call to live mindfully and appreciate our physical senses.

notes.title: Irony of Sight
notes.content: Keller highlights the irony that those with sight often see less than those without it.

facts.fact: Helen Keller lost her sight and hearing at the age of 19 months.
She used her sense of touch to 'see' the world before writing this essay.
The essay was first published in the Atlantic Monthly in 1933.
